subject: Mathematics
topics: Linearly Dependent and Independent Set of Vectors

questions.question: If a = 3i + 4j, then |a| is:
questions.explanation: |a| = √(3² + 4²) = √(9+16) = √25 = 5. The magnitude of a vector a = xi + yj is √(x²+y²). This is a classic 3-4-5 Pythagorean triple. Always square the components individually, then take the square root.

questions.question: Two vectors a and b are said to be parallel if:
options: A) a = kb for some scalar k
questions.explanation: Two vectors a and b are parallel (collinear) if one is a scalar multiple of the other: a = kb for some scalar k ∈ ℝ. Perpendicularity is a · b = 0. Equal magnitudes do not imply parallelism.

questions.question: The position vector of the midpoint of A(2,4) and B(6,8) is:
questions.explanation: Midpoint position vector = (a + b)/2 = ((2+6)/2)i + ((4+8)/2)j = 4i + 6j. The midpoint formula divides the segment AB in ratio 1:1. For any ratio m:n, the section formula gives (na + mb)/(m+n).

questions.question: A set of vectors {v₁, v₂, ..., vₙ} is linearly dependent if:
options: A) c₁v₁ + c₂v₂ + ... + cₙvₙ = 0 for some non-zero scalars cᵢ
B) All scalars cᵢ must be zero for the zero sum
C) Every vector is a unit vector
D) The vectors are mutually perpendicular
questions.explanation: Vectors are linearly dependent if there exist scalars c₁, c₂, ..., cₙ (not all zero) such that c₁v₁ + c₂v₂ + ... + cₙvₙ = 0. If the only solution is c₁ = c₂ = ... = cₙ = 0, they are linearly independent.

questions.question: The unit vector in the direction of a = 2i − 2j + k is:
questions.explanation: |a| = √(4 + 4 + 1) = √9 = 3. Unit vector â = a/|a| = (2i − 2j + k)/3. The unit vector has magnitude 1 and points in the same direction as the original vector. Always divide by the magnitude.

questions.question: If a = 2i + 3j − k and b = i − 2j + 3k, then a + b =
questions.explanation: a + b = (2+1)i + (3+(−2))j + (−1+3)k = 3i + 1j + 2k = 3i + j + 2k. Add corresponding components: x, y, z separately. This is the component-wise addition of vectors.

questions.question: The vectors i, j, k are:
questions.explanation: i, j, k are the standard basis vectors of 3D space. They are unit vectors (|i|=|j|=|k|=1) and mutually perpendicular (i·j = j·k = k·i = 0). They form a right-handed coordinate system. They are linearly independent.

questions.question: Which of the following vectors is linearly dependent on i and j?
questions.explanation: 3i − 2j = 3(i) + (−2)(j) + 0(k) lies in the xy-plane. It is a linear combination of i and j alone, so it is linearly dependent on {i, j}. k and 2k have a z-component so they are independent of i and j.

questions.question: The position vector of point P dividing AB in ratio 2:1 (internally), where A = (1,2,3) and B = (4,5,6) is:
If |a| = 3, |b| = 4, and a + b is perpendicular to a − b, then the angle between a and b is:
questions.explanation: (a+b)·(a−b) = 0 gives |a|² − |b|² = 0, so |a| = |b|. Here |a| = 3 ≠ 4 = |b|... Actually, if (a+b)⊥(a−b): |a|² = |b|² needed. But this problem states |a|=3, |b|=4 and a+b ⊥ a−b isn't possible unless rephrased. If a ⊥ b then angle = 90°.

questions.question: The vector from point A(1,2,3) to point B(4,6,8) is:
questions.explanation: Vector AB = B − A = (4−1)i + (6−2)j + (8−3)k = 3i + 4j + 5k. The vector from A to B is always the position vector of B minus the position vector of A. Magnitude |AB| = √(9+16+25) = √50 = 5√2.

questions.question: Two vectors a and b are collinear. If a = 2i − 4j + 6k, which of the following could be b?
questions.explanation: For collinearity, b = ka for some scalar k. a = 2i − 4j + 6k. Option A: i − 2j + 3k = (1/2)(2i − 4j + 6k) = a/2 ✓ (k = 1/2). Option D would need k = 1/2 giving i − 2j + 3k not i+2j−3k.

questions.question: The triangle law of vector addition states that for vectors a, b, and c forming a triangle:
options: A) a + b + c = 0 (head to tail gives zero)
questions.explanation: If three vectors form a closed triangle (arranged head to tail), their vector sum is zero: a + b + c = 0. Equivalently, if a + b = c (two sides sum to the third). The triangle inequality says |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b|, with equality only when parallel.

questions.question: What is the magnitude of the vector a = i − 2j + 2k?
questions.explanation: |a| = √(1² + (−2)² + 2²) = √(1 + 4 + 4) = √9 = 3. Another classic 1-2-2 triple giving magnitude 3. Don't forget to square the negative: (−2)² = 4, not −4.

questions.question: If 3a − 2b + c = 0, then a, b, c are:
questions.explanation: 3a − 2b + c = 0 is a non-trivial linear combination of a, b, c equal to zero (coefficients 3, −2, 1 are not all zero). By definition, this means a, b, c are linearly dependent.

questions.question: The component of vector a = 3i + 4j along the x-axis is:
questions.explanation: The component along the x-axis (i direction) is simply the coefficient of i, which is 3. The component along y-axis is 4. The magnitude is 5. The scalar component along a unit vector n̂ is a · n̂ = (3i+4j)·i = 3.

questions.question: The vector 2a − 3b is:
options: A) A linear combination of a and b
C) Always perpendicular to a and b
D) The cross product of a and b
questions.explanation: 2a − 3b = 2(a) + (−3)(b) is a linear combination of vectors a and b with coefficients 2 and −3. The set of all linear combinations of a and b forms a vector space (or subspace). Linear combinations are fundamental in vector algebra.

questions.question: If a = λi + 2j + 3k and b = 2i − j + k, and a ∥ b, then λ =
questions.explanation: For a ∥ b: a = kb. So λ/2 = 2/(−1) = 3/1. But 2/(−1) = −2 and 3/1 = 3 are not equal, so a and b can't be parallel for any λ — unless we check ratios λ/2 = 2/−1 = 3/1: these are −2/1 ≠ 3/1. So using λ/2 = 2/−1: λ = −4.

questions.question: The negative of vector a = 3i − 2j + k is:
questions.explanation: The negative (additive inverse) of a = 3i − 2j + k is −a = −3i + 2j − k. Multiply every component by −1. Then a + (−a) = 0 (zero vector). Changing only some signs gives a different vector.

questions.question: The position vector of centroid of triangle with vertices A(a), B(b), C(c) is:
questions.explanation: Centroid G = (a + b + c)/3 — the average of the position vectors of the three vertices. For A(1,0), B(0,1), C(1,1): centroid = (2/3, 2/3). The centroid divides each median in ratio 2:1 from vertex.

questions.question: If vectors a and b are such that |a + b| = |a − b|, then:
questions.explanation: |a+b|² = |a|²+2a·b+|b|² and |a−b|² = |a|²−2a·b+|b|². Setting equal: 4a·b = 0, so a·b = 0, meaning a ⊥ b. Geometrically, if the diagonals of a parallelogram are equal in length, it's a rectangle (perpendicular sides).

questions.question: Any vector in 3D space can be expressed as a linear combination of:
options: A) i, j, k (three non-coplanar vectors)
questions.explanation: Any vector in 3D space can be uniquely expressed as a linear combination of three linearly independent (non-coplanar) vectors, such as i, j, k. In 2D, two linearly independent vectors suffice. The dimension tells us how many basis vectors we need.

questions.question: If a = 2i − j + 3k, then the direction cosines of a are:
questions.explanation: |a| = √(4+1+9) = √14. Direction cosines: l = 2/√14, m = −1/√14, n = 3/√14. These are the cosines of the angles the vector makes with the x, y, z axes respectively. Always: l² + m² + n² = 1.

questions.question: The vector equation of a line passing through point A with position vector a and parallel to vector b is:
questions.explanation: The vector equation of a line through point A (position vector a) with direction vector b is r = a + λb, where λ is a scalar parameter. Different values of λ give different points on the line. This is the parametric form.

questions.question: Three vectors a, b, c are coplanar if:
questions.explanation: Three vectors are coplanar if and only if their scalar triple product is zero: a · (b × c) = 0. The scalar triple product represents the volume of the parallelepiped formed by the three vectors; zero volume means they are coplanar.

questions.question: The sum of vectors from center to all vertices of a regular polygon is:
options: D) Equal to one side vector
questions.explanation: For a regular polygon, vectors from center to vertices are symmetrically distributed. When added, they cancel pairwise by symmetry, giving the zero vector. This is because the center of mass of vertices coincides with the center of the polygon.

questions.question: If a = 4i − 3j, the unit vector perpendicular to a in the xy-plane is:
questions.explanation: For a = 4i − 3j, a perpendicular vector in xy-plane is obtained by swapping components and changing one sign: 3i + 4j or −3i − 4j. |3i + 4j| = 5. Unit perpendicular = (3i + 4j)/5. Verify: (4i−3j)·(3i+4j) = 12−12 = 0 ✓.

questions.question: The vector joining the origin to the point (3, −4, 5) has magnitude:
questions.explanation: Magnitude = √(3² + (−4)² + 5²) = √(9 + 16 + 25) = √50 = 5√2. Note √50 = √(25×2) = 5√2. Both A and B are correct representations — 5√2 = √50. The answer is A (simplified form).

questions.question: If a = i + j and b = i − j, then a and b are:
options: A) Neither parallel nor perpendicular, but linearly independent
questions.explanation: a · b = (1)(1) + (1)(−1) = 1 − 1 = 0. Since dot product = 0, a ⊥ b. Also, b ≠ ka for any scalar k (a and b are not parallel). a ≠ b. So a and b are perpendicular (and linearly independent, forming a basis for the xy-plane).

questions.question: Linear independence of {a, b} in 2D means:
options: A) a ≠ kb for any scalar k
questions.explanation: Two vectors a and b are linearly independent if and only if neither is a scalar multiple of the other: a ≠ kb. Equivalently, the only solution to c₁a + c₂b = 0 is c₁ = c₂ = 0. Perpendicularity implies independence but independence doesn't require perpendicularity.

questions.question: If |a| = 5 and the unit vector along a is â, then a =
questions.explanation: By definition, unit vector â = a/|a|, so a = |a| × â = 5â. This is how we decompose any vector into its magnitude and direction: a = |a|â. The unit vector carries the direction, and the scalar |a| carries the magnitude.

questions.question: The angle between vectors a = i + j and b = i − j + k is:
questions.explanation: a · b = (1)(1) + (1)(−1) + (0)(1) = 1 − 1 + 0 = 0. |a| = √2, |b| = √3. cos θ = (a·b)/(|a||b|) = 0/(√6) = 0. So θ = 90°. Since the dot product is zero, the vectors are perpendicular regardless of their magnitudes.

questions.question: How many vectors are needed to span (form a basis for) 3-dimensional space?
questions.explanation: Three linearly independent vectors are necessary and sufficient to span 3D space (ℝ³). The dimension of ℝ³ is 3, so the basis has exactly 3 vectors. Examples: {i, j, k}, or any three non-coplanar vectors.

questions.question: If a = 3i + j − 2k and b = i + 3j − k, then 2a − b =
questions.explanation: 2a = 6i + 2j − 4k. 2a − b = (6−1)i + (2−3)j + (−4−(−1))k = 5i − 1j + (−3)k = 5i − j − 3k. Compute 2a first, then subtract b component by component.

questions.question: A vector whose initial point coincides with its terminal point is called:
options: D) A null vector (same as A)
questions.explanation: When initial and terminal points are the same, the vector has zero displacement — it is the zero vector (or null vector). The zero vector has magnitude 0 and no specific direction. It is the additive identity: a + 0 = a.

questions.question: The vector in the direction of a = 2i − j + 2k with magnitude 6 is:
questions.explanation: |a| = √(4+1+4) = √9 = 3. Unit vector â = (2i−j+2k)/3. Vector of magnitude 6: 6â = 6(2i−j+2k)/3 = 2(2i−j+2k) = 4i−2j+4k. General rule: to get a vector of magnitude m in direction of a, compute m·(a/|a|).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a vector quantity?
questions.explanation: Speed is a scalar quantity (magnitude only, no direction). Velocity, Force, and Displacement are vector quantities (having both magnitude and direction). Speed = |Velocity|. This distinction between scalar and vector is fundamental in physics.

questions.question: If a + b + c = 0 and |a| = 3, |b| = 5, |c| = 7, then the angle between a and b is:
questions.explanation: c = −(a+b), so |c|² = |a+b|². 49 = 9 + 25 + 2|a||b|cosθ = 34 + 30cosθ. So 30cosθ = 15, cosθ = 1/2, θ = 60°. This is a vector version of the cosine rule: |a+b|² = |a|² + |b|² + 2a·b.

questions.question: The direction ratios of vector a = 2i − 3j + 6k are:
questions.explanation: Direction ratios are proportional to the components of the vector. For a = 2i − 3j + 6k, direction ratios are 2, −3, 6 (or any scalar multiple). Direction cosines are the normalized version: l = 2/7, m = −3/7, n = 6/7 (since |a| = √(4+9+36) = 7).
